content:
9 min read
Just now

--

Last Tuesday I was reviewing a pull request from one of the strongest developers on our team. Clean variable names, consistent formatting, proper separation of concerns, XML doc comments on the public API. The kind of PR that makes you nod while scrolling.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

It was also 400 lines long.
Not because the feature was complex — it was a domain model for customer orders, a service class that processed them, and a small utility for calculating discount tiers. I’ve written that exact thing a dozen times. The code wasn’t bloated because the developer was careless. It was bloated because they were writing C# the way C# was taught five years ago.
I left a single comment on the PR: “Mind if I show you something?”
I rewrote the entire thing in 12 lines. Not by cutting corners. Not by writing clever one-liners that nobody can read at 9 AM. Just by using the language features that have been sitting in C# for years, waiting for developers to notice them.
If your C# still looks like it was written in 2018, this article is for you.
Here’s what the core domain model looked like in the original PR:
// The "before" — 47 lines just for this one class. (.NET 6 and earlier style)
public class CustomerOrder
{
private readonly List<OrderItem> _items…
